hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_desc: Quote prices, build packages, and negotiate deals using the first conditional -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Prices, Packages & Deals
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 9 -- Prices, Packages & Deals

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, this is Miguel from the Grand Coast Hotel. Thank you for your enquiry about our group rates for next season. Here is a quick summary. If you book more than thirty rooms, we will offer you our best net rate, with a ten percent markup instead of the usual fifteen. During peak season, a small surcharge applies at the weekend, but if you travel in the low season, that surcharge disappears completely. We ask for a twenty percent deposit to confirm any group, and our cancellation policy is flexible: if you cancel thirty days in advance, you pay nothing. Breakfast is always complimentary. Call me back and we will finalise the package.

Welcome to this short segment on smart negotiating. Here is one simple rule that every good salesperson knows: never give a discount for nothing. Always ask for something in return. In English, we do this with the first conditional. You say, if you book early, I will offer a better rate, or if you commit to thirty rooms, we will waive the surcharge. Notice the pattern: if plus the present simple for the condition, and will plus the verb for the result. This little structure protects your prices and makes the client feel they have earned the deal. So next time you talk numbers, remember: state your condition first, then your offer.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
the base price before any markup is added
b
the extra amount added to the base price to make a profit
c
a special reduced price for a large number of people
d
money paid in advance to hold a booking
e
the rules for changing or cancelling a booking
f
the busiest and most expensive time of the year
g
the quietest and cheapest time of the year
h
several services combined and sold at one price
i
an extra charge added to the basic price
j
to state the price for a service
k
free, included at no extra cost
l
a reduction in the normal price

reading.rtitle: The Art of the Deal in Tourism
paras: In the travel business, a price is never just a number. Behind every quote there is a story. The hotel gives the travel company a net rate -- the base price. The company then adds a markup and sells it to the client at a higher, gross price. The difference is how the business survives. A good commercial director knows these numbers by heart and can build a package that is attractive to the client and still profitable for the company.

Prices also change with the calendar. In peak season, when everyone wants to travel, hotels are full and rates rise; a small surcharge may even apply on busy weekends. In the low season, the same room can cost far less, and a smart buyer books early to lock in the best rate. This is why timing is everything: the same package can have two very different prices, depending only on the month.

The real skill, though, is in the conversation. Experienced negotiators rarely give a discount for free. Instead, they trade: 'If you book forty rooms, I will hold the low-season rate.' 'If you pay the deposit today, breakfast will be complimentary.' This is the first conditional, and it is the natural language of every deal. It turns a flat price into a flexible agreement, and it leaves both sides feeling they have won.
reading.source: Adapted from a hospitality business guide, The Commercial Edge

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Hotel prices in tourism never change and are the same all year.
In tourism, prices depend on markup and season, and negotiators use the first conditional to trade offers.
Good negotiators always give discounts for free to keep clients happy.

items: The net rate is the base price the hotel gives the travel company.
The first paragraph says the hotel gives the company a net rate -- the base price.
Prices in tourism stay the same in every season.
In peak season rates rise; in the low season the same room can cost far less.
Experienced negotiators often give discounts for free.
The text says they rarely give a discount for free; instead they trade a condition for an offer.
'If you book forty rooms, I will hold the rate' is an example of the first conditional.
The third paragraph gives this exact sentence as an example of the first conditional.

gapfill.text: Remember the first conditional: 'if' + present simple (the condition), then 'will' + verb (the result). 'If you book early, I will offer a discount.'

bank.label: Useful language for prices and deals
items: Could you quote me a group rate?
If you book early, I will offer a discount.
The net rate is our base price.
A small surcharge applies in peak season.
We ask for a twenty percent deposit.
If you confirm today, I will hold this rate.

lf.title: Complete the first conditional
items: early, I will offer a discount.
If you pay the deposit, breakfast
in the low season, prices fall.

lf.text: The 'if' part uses the present simple (book, confirm, travel); the result part uses 'will' + verb (will hold, will be).

items: A client wants a discount. Offer one using the first conditional: 'If you..., I will...'.
The client asks about cancellations. Explain your policy with an 'if' sentence.
It is peak season. Negotiate a better rate by trading a condition for an offer.
Quote a full package (rate, deposit, what is included) and close the deal.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = net, 2 = markup, 3 = peak, 4 = surcharge, 5 = trade.
Condition: 'If you book forty rooms...' (present simple after 'if').
Result: '...I will hold the low-season rate.' (will + verb).
Never say 'If you will book'. Say 'If you book, I will...'.
answerkey.note: First conditional: if + present simple, then will + verb. The 'if' part never takes 'will'.
